subject: Chemistry
title: Isotopes: Same Element, Different Weight
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Isotopes: Same Element, Different Weight.

hook.text: Carbon-12 and Carbon-14 are the same element with the same chemistry—but different masses. They're isotopes.

buildup.text: Isotopes have the same number of protons (same element) but different numbers of neutrons (different mass).

discovery.text: Frederick Soddy discovered isotopes in 1913 while studying radioactive decay chains of thorium and uranium.

twist.text: Carbon-14 is radioactive with a half-life of 5,730 years. Archaeologists use it to date ancient objects up to 50,000 years old.

climax.text: Hydrogen has three isotopes: protium (0 neutrons), deuterium (1 neutron), and tritium (2 neutrons, radioactive).

punchline.text: Same protons, different neutrons. Same chemistry, different stability.

quiz.question: What makes two atoms isotopes of the same element?
options: Same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons
Same number of electrons but different protons
Same atomic mass but different chemical properties
